summary: O'Neil pushes back against the viral framing of a story where an economist was questioned on a plane for writing equations. She argues the real story isn't about math being under attack, but about an irrational passenger who reported a man for doing math while appearing 'swarthy.' The airline's response of removing and questioning the economist was standard procedure once terrorism was invoked, but the instigating complaint was absurd. O'Neil criticizes the media's framing while noting her New York-honed instinct for recognizing irrational behavior in public.
key_points: The media framed this as 'math under attack' when it was really about one paranoid passenger's irrational behavior
The airline followed standard protocol once terrorism allegations were made, which O'Neil finds understandable
The passenger's behavior (passing notes, reporting math as suspicious) marked her as the actual problem
Racial profiling played a role - the man was described as 'swarthy Italian'
Urban experience makes you better at recognizing that seemingly normal people can be 'utterly insane'
money_quotes: The article's headline should have read: Crazy Woman Removed From Plane
Some paranoid freak decided that an integral sign on the page of notes of a swarthy Italian man next to her posed a terrorist threat.
I'm just so used to sitting next to perfectly normal looking people on the subway who turn out to be utterly insane, that I'm ready to assume anyone at all is fucking nuts.
Note-passers older than 13 are already suspect; get up and walk over to someone if you're worried.
Some woman was nuts, she indicated as much to the flight crew, and they removed her from the flight. Math isn't under attack.
key_insight: When irrational people invoke terrorism to harass others, the story isn't about what they feared—it's about their irrationality.
